stem: A university's plagiarism detection software flags plagiarized work with 92% accuracy. The test also has a specificity of 80% (correctly identifying negatives). A colleague presents this data and concludes: 'Since the test is 86% accurate, when it flags someone, there's a 86% chance they're actually plagiarized.' The base rate of submissions that are actually plagiarized in this student submissions pool is estimated between 0.80% and 2.60%. Is your colleague's reasoning correct? What is the actual probability that a flagged student submission is truly plagiarized? How does the uncertain base rate affect your answer?

ground_truth.answer: The colleague is wrong — this is the base rate fallacy. P(plagiarized | flagged) ranges from 0.0335 to 0.103 (NOT 86%). The vast majority of flagged cases are false positives because the base rate is so low. Point estimate at 1.70%: 0.0692.

solution_steps: The colleague confuses P(flagged | plagiarized) = 0.86 with P(plagiarized | flagged).
Apply Bayes' theorem: PPV = sensitivity × base_rate / (sensitivity × base_rate + (1-specificity) × (1-base_rate)).
The true PPV (0.0692) is dramatically lower than the naive estimate (0.86) because when the base rate is low, false positives dominate.
common_failure_modes: Agreeing with the colleague that the probability is 86% (the base rate fallacy)
Confusing test accuracy (sensitivity) with positive predictive value (PPV)
Not recognizing that low base rates make most positive flags false positives
Providing a single PPV without accounting for uncertainty in the base rate
